title: Linux
description: From first shell commands to systemd and real troubleshooting: become genuinely at home on any Linux box.

sections.title: The Terminal & Shell Fluency
sections.description: Get comfortable living in the terminal: what Linux is, how the shell works, and how to compose commands.

topics.title: What Linux Is
topics.description: Linux is a kernel plus a userland of tools, packaged into distributions like Debian, Ubuntu, Fedora, and Arch.
topics.why: Knowing the kernel/userspace split and how distros differ explains why commands, paths, and package tools vary between systems. It also demystifies where 'Linux' ends and GNU tools, shells, and desktop environments begin.
learn: Explain the difference between the kernel, the shell, and the distribution
Pick one distro (Ubuntu or Fedora) and install it in a VM or use WSL2
Run uname -a and cat /etc/os-release and interpret every field
Skim the history: Unix, GNU, and the Linux kernel
criteria: You can explain kernel vs userspace and what a distribution adds
You have a working Linux environment (VM, WSL2, or bare metal) you can experiment in
You can identify your distro and kernel version from the command line

topics.title: Terminal & Navigation Basics
topics.description: The shell (usually bash) reads your commands, and a handful of verbs — pwd, cd, ls, cat, less — let you move around and inspect the system.
topics.why: Everything else in Linux builds on fast, confident navigation. Muscle memory for paths, tab completion, and history turns the terminal from intimidating to faster than any GUI.
learn: Navigate with pwd, cd, ls -la and explain absolute vs relative paths
View files with cat, less, head, and tail
Use Tab completion, Ctrl+R history search, and arrow-key history constantly
Learn shortcuts: ~, -, .., and Ctrl+C / Ctrl+L / Ctrl+D

resources.label: MIT Missing Semester — Course overview + the shell

criteria: You can navigate anywhere on the system without a file manager
You reflexively use Tab completion and Ctrl+R instead of retyping
You can explain what the prompt, $PATH, and the working directory are

topics.title: Getting Help: man & friends
topics.description: Linux ships its own documentation: man pages, --help flags, and info documents describe nearly every command and config file.
topics.why: Nobody memorizes every flag — professionals look things up fast. Reading man pages fluently makes you self-sufficient on any machine, including servers with no internet access.
learn: Read a man page end to end (try man ls) and learn to search inside it with /
Understand man sections: 1 commands, 5 file formats, 8 admin tools
Use command --help for quick syntax reminders
Find commands you don't know the name of with man -k / apropos
criteria: You can answer your own 'what flag does X' questions from man pages
You can explain what man 5 passwd means vs man 1 passwd
You reach for --help or man before searching the web

topics.title: Pipes, Redirection & Composition
topics.description: Every process has stdin, stdout, and stderr; pipes and redirection let you chain small tools into powerful one-liners.
topics.why: Composition is the core idea of Unix — mastering | , >, >>, and 2> is what separates typing commands from wielding the shell. It unlocks log analysis, data munging, and ad-hoc automation with zero extra software.
learn: Explain stdin, stdout, stderr and file descriptors 0, 1, 2
Redirect output with >, >>, 2>, and &> and input with <
Chain tools with pipes: sort, uniq -c, wc -l, cut, tr, head
Use tee to watch and save output at the same time
Combine commands with &&, ||, and ; and explain exit codes

resources.label: MIT Missing Semester — Shell tools and scripting

criteria: You can build a pipeline that answers 'top 10 most frequent lines in this file'
You can redirect stderr and stdout to different places on purpose
You completed at least 10 levels of OverTheWire Bandit

sections.title: The Filesystem
sections.description: Learn how Linux organizes everything as files, and how to create, find, link, and archive them efficiently.

topics.title: Filesystem Hierarchy (FHS)
topics.description: Linux lays out the system in a standard tree: /etc for config, /var for changing data, /usr for installed software, /home for users, and more.
topics.why: Knowing where things live means you can find config files, logs, and binaries on any machine in seconds. It's the mental map every troubleshooting session starts from.
learn: Tour the top-level directories and state what each is for: /etc, /var, /usr, /home, /tmp, /opt, /proc, /dev
Explain why /proc and /dev contain 'files' that aren't on disk
Locate the config file and log file for one service you use (e.g. sshd)
Read man hier for the canonical description
criteria: You can say where config, logs, binaries, and user data live without looking it up
You can explain the difference between /bin, /usr/bin, and /usr/local/bin
You found a real service's config and logs using only the hierarchy

topics.title: Creating, Copying & Deleting Files
topics.description: cp, mv, rm, mkdir, and touch manipulate files and directories, with globbing patterns (*, ?, [ ]) selecting groups of them.
topics.why: These verbs are the hands of the shell — every deploy, backup, and cleanup uses them. Understanding globbing and the sharp edges of rm keeps you fast and safe.
learn: Create, copy, move, and rename files and whole directory trees (cp -r, mv)
Delete carefully: rm, rm -r, and why rm -rf demands a second look
Use globs (*.log, file?.txt, [0-9]*) and brace expansion (file{1..5}.txt)
Understand hidden dotfiles and how ls -a reveals them
criteria: You can reorganize a messy directory tree entirely from the shell
You can predict what a glob will match before pressing Enter
You can explain why rm has no trash can and how to mitigate that

topics.title: Finding Files & Searching Text
topics.description: find locates files by name, size, time, or type; grep searches inside files with patterns; xargs feeds results into other commands.
topics.why: 'Where is that config?' and 'which file mentions this error?' are daily questions. find + grep + xargs answer them on any system, no IDE required.
learn: Use find by name, type, mtime, and size (find /var/log -name '*.log' -mtime -1)
Search file contents with grep -r, -i, -n, -v and basic regex
Chain find results into commands with -exec and xargs
Practice regular expressions until character classes and anchors feel natural
criteria: You can find every file changed in the last day under a directory
You can grep a codebase or /etc for a string with the right flags first try
You built a find | xargs pipeline that transforms many files at once

topics.title: Links, Inodes & File Metadata
topics.description: Files are inodes — metadata plus data blocks — and names are just links to them; ln creates hard links and symlinks that point at the same content.
topics.why: Symlinks power version switching (like /usr/bin/python), config management, and deploys. Understanding inodes explains weird phenomena like deleted files still consuming disk space.
learn: Inspect metadata with stat and ls -li and identify the inode number
Create hard links and symlinks with ln and ln -s and compare their behavior
Break a symlink on purpose and recognize how it fails
Explain why a deleted-but-open file still uses disk until the process exits
criteria: You can explain hard link vs symlink and when each is appropriate
You can read stat output: size, inode, link count, timestamps
You used a symlink to switch between two versions of something

topics.title: Archives & Compression
topics.description: tar bundles trees of files into one archive; gzip, xz, and zstd compress them — the .tar.gz you meet everywhere.
topics.why: Backups, releases, and log rotation all speak tar and gzip. Being able to pack and unpack archives without googling the flags is table stakes for server work.
learn: Create and extract tarballs: tar -czf and tar -xzf, plus -t to list contents
Compress and decompress single files with gzip/gunzip and xz
Extract only part of an archive and extract into a specific directory (-C)
Compare compression tools (gzip vs xz vs zstd) on speed and ratio
criteria: You can create and extract a .tar.gz from memory
You can list an archive's contents before extracting it
You backed up and restored a real directory tree with tar

sections.title: Users, Groups & Permissions
sections.description: Understand Linux's multi-user model: who can read, write, and execute what, and how privilege is granted safely.

topics.title: Users & Groups
topics.description: Every process and file belongs to a user and group, defined in /etc/passwd and /etc/group and managed with useradd, usermod, and passwd.
topics.why: The user model underpins all Linux security — services run as unprivileged users, and access is granted through group membership. You can't reason about permissions until you know who 'you' are.
learn: Inspect identity with id, whoami, and groups
Read /etc/passwd and /etc/group and explain each field
Create a user and a group, and add the user to the group
Switch users with su - and explain login vs non-login shells
criteria: You can explain every field of an /etc/passwd line
You created a new user and group and verified membership with id
You can explain why services run as their own users

topics.title: File Permissions: chmod & chown
topics.description: The rwx permission triplets for user, group, and other control access to every file; chmod changes them and chown changes ownership.
topics.why: Permission errors are among the most common Linux problems, and misconfigured permissions are a top security hole. Reading rwxr-xr-x at a glance and fixing it correctly is a daily skill.
learn: Decode ls -l output: type, permission triplets, owner, group
Change permissions both ways: symbolic (u+x, go-w) and octal (644, 755)
Change ownership with chown user:group and use -R with care
Explain umask and why new files aren't created executable
Know why directories need x to be entered, not just r
criteria: You can translate between rwxr-x--- and 750 instantly
You diagnosed and fixed a real 'Permission denied' error
You can explain what x means on a directory vs a file

topics.title: root, sudo & Least Privilege
topics.description: root is the all-powerful superuser; sudo grants controlled, logged, temporary root access configured through /etc/sudoers.
topics.why: Running everything as root is how systems get destroyed by typos and attackers alike. sudo lets you do admin work safely, and understanding it is essential for any shared or production machine.
learn: Use sudo for single commands and sudo -i for a root shell, and know the difference
Edit sudo config safely with visudo, never by editing /etc/sudoers directly
Grant a user or group specific commands via sudoers rules
Explain the principle of least privilege and where sudo actions get logged
criteria: You can explain why sudo beats logging in as root
You wrote a sudoers rule granting one specific command
You know where to find the log of who ran what with sudo

topics.title: Special Permissions: setuid, setgid, sticky
topics.description: Three extra permission bits change execution and ownership rules: setuid runs a program as its owner, setgid shares group ownership, and the sticky bit protects shared directories like /tmp.
topics.why: These bits explain how passwd changes a root-owned file as a normal user, and how team directories keep consistent group ownership. They're also a classic privilege-escalation vector, so security work requires spotting them.
learn: Find setuid binaries on your system: find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
Explain why /usr/bin/passwd needs setuid and what the s in its permissions means
Set setgid on a shared directory and watch new files inherit the group
Explain the sticky bit on /tmp (the t in drwxrwxrwt)
criteria: You can explain setuid, setgid, and sticky with a real example of each
You built a setgid shared directory and verified group inheritance
You can say why unexpected setuid binaries are a security red flag

sections.title: Processes & System Resources
sections.description: See what the machine is actually doing: how processes live and die, and how to monitor CPU, memory, and disk.

topics.title: Processes & the /proc Filesystem
topics.description: Every running program is a process with a PID, a parent, an environment, and open files — all visible through /proc and standard tools.
topics.why: Processes are the unit of everything on Linux: services, shells, and scripts. Understanding fork/exec, parent-child trees, and /proc turns 'the server is doing something weird' into a question you can answer.
learn: Explain PID, PPID, and the process tree; view it with ps -ef and pstree
Describe the fork/exec model of how new processes start
Explore /proc/<pid>: cmdline, environ, fd, status
Explain zombie and orphan processes and why they exist
criteria: You can trace any process back to its parent up to PID 1
You inspected a live process's open files and environment via /proc
You can explain what a zombie process is and who reaps it

topics.title: Monitoring: ps, top & Load
topics.description: ps snapshots processes; top and htop show live CPU and memory usage; uptime's load average summarizes how busy the system is.
topics.why: 'Why is the server slow?' starts here. Reading top and load averages correctly tells you in seconds whether you're CPU-bound, memory-bound, or fine — and which process is to blame.
learn: Master one ps incantation (ps aux or ps -ef) and read every column
Drive top/htop: sort by CPU and memory, filter, and kill from inside
Interpret load average relative to CPU core count
Read memory correctly with free -h, including buffers/cache vs available

resources.label: Wizard Zines — Julia Evans' systems comics

criteria: You can find the top CPU and memory consumers in under 30 seconds
You can explain a load average of 8.0 on a 4-core machine
You can explain why 'free' memory being low is usually fine

topics.title: Signals & Job Control
topics.description: Signals like SIGTERM, SIGKILL, and SIGINT are how processes are asked (or forced) to stop; job control (&, Ctrl+Z, fg, bg) manages processes from your shell.
topics.why: Killing processes correctly — graceful first, forceful last — prevents data corruption and understands why some processes won't die. Job control lets you juggle long-running tasks in a single terminal or over SSH.
learn: Send signals with kill, killall, and pkill; know TERM (15) vs KILL (9) vs HUP
Explain what Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Z actually send
Manage jobs: command &, jobs, fg, bg, and disown
Keep processes alive after logout with nohup (and know tmux exists)
criteria: You always try SIGTERM before SIGKILL and can explain why
You can suspend, background, and resume a job in one terminal
You can explain why kill -9 can't kill a process in uninterruptible sleep

topics.title: Disk & Resource Usage
topics.description: df shows filesystem capacity, du shows what's eating space, and nice/ionice control how much CPU and I/O priority a process gets.
topics.why: Full disks are the most common cause of mysterious outages — services fail in strange ways when they can't write. Finding and clearing space fast, and prioritizing workloads, are core operations skills.
learn: Check filesystem usage with df -h and inode usage with df -i
Hunt space hogs with du -sh * and du -h --max-depth=1 | sort -h
Explain why df and du can disagree (deleted-but-open files)
Lower a heavy job's priority with nice and renice
criteria: You can find the biggest directories on a full disk in under a minute
You can explain a full-inodes failure even when df shows free space
You resolved a df/du mismatch by finding a deleted-but-open file

sections.title: Package Management
sections.description: Install, update, and remove software the right way with your distro's package manager — and know the alternatives.

topics.title: Packages, Repositories & Dependencies
topics.description: Distros ship software as packages — signed archives with metadata and dependency lists — pulled from online repositories by a package manager.
topics.why: Package managers are why Linux systems stay consistent, updatable, and secure. Understanding repos, dependencies, and signing explains both the power of apt/dnf and what goes wrong when you bypass them.
learn: Explain what a package contains: files, metadata, dependencies, scripts
Explain repositories, mirrors, and why packages are cryptographically signed
Describe dependency resolution and what 'dependency hell' means
Map the ecosystems: deb/apt (Debian, Ubuntu) vs rpm/dnf (Fedora, RHEL) vs pacman (Arch)
criteria: You can explain what happens end-to-end when you install a package
You can name your distro's package format, manager, and repo config location
You can explain why installing random binaries from the web is riskier than packages

topics.title: apt on Debian & Ubuntu
topics.description: apt is the front-end for Debian-family package management: update indexes, install, upgrade, remove, and search, with dpkg underneath.
topics.why: Debian and Ubuntu run a huge share of servers and containers, so apt fluency pays off almost everywhere. Knowing the update/upgrade rhythm and how to inspect packages keeps systems patched and debuggable.
learn: Run the core loop: apt update, apt upgrade, apt install, apt remove/purge
Search and inspect: apt search, apt show, apt list --installed
Answer 'which package owns this file?' with dpkg -S and list contents with dpkg -L
Understand /etc/apt/sources.list and what adding a repository means
criteria: You can explain the difference between apt update and apt upgrade
You installed, inspected, and cleanly removed a package including its config
You traced a file on disk back to the package that owns it

topics.title: dnf on Fedora & RHEL
topics.description: dnf manages rpm packages on Fedora, RHEL, and derivatives, with the same install/upgrade/search workflow plus rpm for low-level queries.
topics.why: Enterprise Linux (RHEL, Rocky, Alma, Amazon Linux) dominates corporate servers, and it speaks rpm/dnf. Being bilingual across apt and dnf means no environment slows you down.
learn: Run the core loop: dnf install, dnf upgrade, dnf remove, dnf search, dnf info
Query installed packages with rpm -qa and file ownership with rpm -qf
List and enable repositories with dnf repolist
Review transaction history and roll back with dnf history
criteria: You can perform install/search/remove on a Fedora-family system without notes
You can map each apt command you know to its dnf equivalent
You used dnf history to inspect or undo a transaction

topics.title: Building from Source & Universal Packages
topics.description: Some software arrives as source tarballs (./configure && make && make install) or as distro-agnostic formats like Flatpak, Snap, and AppImage.
topics.why: Not everything is packaged for your distro, and sometimes you need a newer version than the repos offer. Knowing how to build from source — and where it puts files — keeps your system comprehensible instead of littered.
learn: Build a small project from source and install it under /usr/local
Explain what ./configure, make, and make install each do
Install something as a Flatpak and explain the sandboxing trade-offs
Know why mixing 'make install' into package-managed paths causes conflicts
criteria: You compiled and installed a real program from a source tarball
You can explain why /usr/local exists and what belongs there
You can compare native packages vs Flatpak/Snap in two sentences

sections.title: Shell Scripting & Automation
sections.description: Turn command-line knowledge into reusable, robust scripts and scheduled automation.

topics.title: Bash Scripting Basics
topics.description: A shell script is a file of commands with a shebang line (#!/usr/bin/env bash), variables, and command substitution, made runnable with chmod +x.
topics.why: Scripts capture what you'd otherwise retype — setup steps, deploys, backups — and make them repeatable and shareable. This is the gateway from using Linux to automating it.
learn: Write a script with a shebang, make it executable, and run it from $PATH
Use variables, $(command) substitution, and arithmetic $(( ))
Read arguments with $1, $#, and "$@" and prompt for input with read
Return meaningful exit codes and check them with $?

resources.label: MIT Missing Semester — Shell tools and scripting

criteria: You wrote a script that takes arguments and does something useful
You can explain what the shebang line does
Your scripts exit non-zero on failure

topics.title: Conditionals, Loops & Functions
topics.description: if/[[ ]], for, while, case, and functions give bash real programming structure for branching on files, strings, and exit codes.
topics.why: Real automation needs decisions: retry on failure, loop over servers, branch on environment. Control flow plus functions turns fragile one-liners into maintainable tools.
learn: Branch with if and [[ ]] tests: string compare, -f/-d file tests, numeric -eq/-lt
Loop over files and lines safely: for f in *.log and while read -r line
Dispatch with case for argument handling
Extract repeated logic into functions with local variables and return codes
criteria: You wrote a script with branching, a loop, and at least one function
You can loop over filenames with spaces without breaking
You can explain the difference between [ ] and [[ ]]

topics.title: Robust Scripts: Quoting, set -e & ShellCheck
topics.description: Defensive bash means quoting every expansion, failing fast with set -euo pipefail, and linting with ShellCheck to catch bugs before they run.
topics.why: Unquoted variables and ignored failures are how scripts delete the wrong directory at 2 a.m. A small set of habits makes shell scripts trustworthy enough for production.
learn: Quote all expansions ("$var", "$@") and explain word splitting and globbing bugs
Start scripts with set -euo pipefail and understand what each flag does
Run ShellCheck on every script and fix what it reports
Add traps for cleanup (trap 'rm -f "$tmp"' EXIT) and use mktemp for temp files

resources.label: Wizard Zines — Bite Size Bash and friends

criteria: Your scripts pass ShellCheck with no warnings
You can demonstrate a concrete bug caused by an unquoted variable
You can explain what set -e, -u, and -o pipefail each catch

topics.title: cron & Scheduled Tasks
topics.description: cron runs commands on a schedule defined in crontab entries; systemd timers are the modern alternative with logging and dependency support.
topics.why: Backups, cleanups, report generation, and certificate renewals all run unattended. Scheduling work correctly — with sane environments and logged output — is automation's last mile.
learn: Read and write the five-field cron syntax (minute hour day month weekday)
Manage your schedule with crontab -e and crontab -l
Handle cron's gotchas: minimal PATH, no interactive environment, capturing output to a log
Compare cron with systemd timers and know when to prefer each
criteria: You can write the cron expression for 'every weekday at 6:30 am' unaided
You scheduled a real script and verified it ran by checking its log
You can name two environment differences between cron and your shell

sections.title: systemd, Networking & Troubleshooting
sections.description: Manage services with systemd, read the journal, inspect the network, and debug real problems with a repeatable method.

topics.title: systemd & systemctl
topics.description: systemd is PID 1 on most distros: it boots the system, supervises services, and exposes control through systemctl.
topics.why: Nearly every 'start/stop/restart the service' task on modern Linux goes through systemctl. Knowing units, states, and enable-vs-start is the difference between administering a server and poking at it.
learn: Control services: systemctl start/stop/restart/reload and read systemctl status
Explain enabled vs started, and wire boot behavior with enable/disable
List units with systemctl list-units and inspect one with systemctl cat
Name the main unit types: service, socket, timer, target, mount
criteria: You can start, stop, enable, and check any service without notes
You can read every section of systemctl status output
You can explain why enabled and running are independent

topics.title: Writing Service Units & Timers
topics.description: Unit files in /etc/systemd/system declare how to run a service — ExecStart, User, Restart policy, dependencies — and timers replace many cron jobs.
topics.why: Turning your script or app into a proper service gets you supervised restarts, ordered startup, resource limits, and journal logging for free. It's how software becomes production-grade on Linux.
learn: Write a minimal .service file with [Unit], [Service], and [Install] sections
Run it as a dedicated user with Restart=on-failure, then daemon-reload and enable it
Express dependencies and ordering with After= and Wants=
Create a .timer unit paired with a service and compare it to cron
criteria: You wrote a working .service unit for one of your own scripts or apps
Your service survives crashes via its Restart policy and runs as a non-root user
You created a timer unit and verified its schedule with systemctl list-timers

topics.title: Logs: journalctl & /var/log
topics.description: The systemd journal collects logs from services and the kernel, queried with journalctl; classic text logs still live in /var/log.
topics.why: Logs are the primary evidence in every incident. Filtering the journal by unit, time, and priority — instead of scrolling — is what makes root-causing fast.
learn: Follow a service's logs: journalctl -u <unit> -f
Filter by time (--since, --until) and priority (-p err), and check the last boot (-b)
Read kernel messages with dmesg or journalctl -k
Tour /var/log and explain log rotation with logrotate
criteria: You can pull all errors from one service in a given time window in one command
You used journal logs to explain why a service failed to start
You can say which logs live in the journal vs /var/log on your system

topics.title: Network Inspection: ip, ss & DNS
topics.description: ip shows addresses and routes, ss shows listening ports and connections, and ping/curl/dig test reachability, HTTP, and DNS.
topics.why: 'Is it the network?' is half of all troubleshooting. A tight loop — link, IP, route, DNS, port, service — localizes connectivity failures in minutes instead of guesswork.
learn: Read interfaces and routes with ip addr and ip route
Find what's listening with ss -tlnp and match ports to processes
Test layer by layer: ping the gateway, ping an IP, resolve with dig, fetch with curl -v
Explain what /etc/resolv.conf and /etc/hosts do
criteria: You can list your machine's IP, gateway, and DNS servers in three commands
You can identify which process owns any listening port
You can bisect 'the site is down' into DNS vs routing vs service in minutes

topics.title: A Troubleshooting Methodology
topics.description: A repeatable loop — observe symptoms, check resources (USE: utilization, saturation, errors), read logs, form one hypothesis, test it — beats random command-flailing.
topics.why: Tools without method produce noise. A disciplined process makes you the person who calmly finds root causes during incidents, and it transfers to every system you'll ever debug.
learn: Adopt a first-five-minutes checklist: uptime, dmesg -T | tail, df -h, free -h, top, journalctl -p err -b
Apply the USE method to CPU, memory, disk, and network in order
Go deeper when logs are silent: trace syscalls with strace and list open files with lsof
Change one thing at a time and keep notes of what you ruled out
Do a mini post-mortem after each incident: root cause, fix, prevention

resources.label: MIT Missing Semester — Debugging and profiling
Wizard Zines — Julia Evans' debugging zines

criteria: You can run your first-five-minutes checklist from memory
You diagnosed a real or simulated outage and wrote down the root cause
You can explain the USE method and apply it to a slow server
